mission.title: Install and Configure Kepler on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kepler is a Prometheus exporter designed to measure energy consumption metrics at the container, pod, and node levels in Kubernetes clusters. Installing Kepler allows you to monitor and optimize energy usage within your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Kepler Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Kepler Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kepler https://sustainable-computing-io.github.io/kepler-helm-chart
```

steps.title: Install Kepler using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Kepler in a new namespace called 'kepler' with the following command:
```bash
helm install kepler kepler/kepler --namespace kepler --create-namespace --version v0.11.4
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Kepler pods are running successfully by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kepler
```

steps.title: Expose the Kepler service
steps.description:
To access the Kepler service, you can port-forward it to your local machine:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/kepler 8080:80 --namespace kepler
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kepler will show the Kepler pods running in the 'kepler' namespace. You can access the Kepler service locally via port 8080 and have configured resource limits for optimal performance.
codeSnippets: helm install kepler kepler/kepler --namespace kepler --create-namespace --version v0.11.4

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To uninstall Kepler, first remove the Helm release using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall kepler --namespace kepler
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, clean up any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd keplers.kepler.sustainable-computing.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'kepler' namespace to remove all associated resources and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kepler
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Kepler installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kepler -o yaml > kepler-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kepler kepler/kepler --namespace kepler --version v0.12.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Kepler pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kepler
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kepler pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kepler
``` 
Look for any configuration issues or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Kepler service, ensure that the port-forwarding command is running and check the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kepler
``` 
Make sure the service is correctly configured and the pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: High resource usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high CPU or memory usage, check the resource limits set for the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kepler --namespace kepler
``` 
Consider adjusting the resource limits if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to missing CRDs, verify that the CRDs were installed correctly:
```bash
kubectl get crd --namespace kepler
``` 
If they are missing, reinstall the Helm chart to ensure all resources are created.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
